                                                                                    ERRATUM
                                       The employment by occupation data which appeared in the March 1967 issue of this publi-
                                       cation (pp. 14-15 and pp. 39-41) were not correctly adjusted to exclude 14 and 15 year-
                                       oldso Similarly, • the unemployment rates by occupation (appearing on pp. 28-31 of the same
                                       issue) contain a few e r r o r s . The entire package of employment and unemployment data by
                                       occupation is now being examined; corrected historical series will be published in the June
                                       1967 issue.



                                                                                  NOTE
                                      Periodically, the Bureau adjusts the industry employment series to a recent benchmark to improve
                                 their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect the hours and earnings series because employment
                                 levels are used as weights. Data from April 1965 forward are subject to revision at the time of the
                                 next benchmark adjustment. Beginning with September 1966 and subsequent issues of Employment and
                                 Earnings and Monthly Report on the Labor Force, national data in Sections B, C, and D are based on
                                 March 1965 benchmarks. Comparable data for prior periods are published in Employment and Earnings
                                 Statistics for the United States, 1909-66, BLS Bulletin 1312-4.
                                      For further information regarding benchmark adjustments and other aspects of the program, the user
                                 is referred to the technical note at the back of this volume.

                    1
                        Quarterly data included in February, May, August, and November issues.

                                       SEASONAL ADJUSTMENT OF RETAIL TRADE EMPLOYMENT

                                   The data for retail trade employment presented in the preceding analysis
                               and the B-tables beginning on page 37 were seasonally adjusted in accordance
                               with factors published in the September 1966 issue of this report. The factors
                               for March and April used to adjust for the unusually early incidence of Easter
                               in 1967, are being restudied. This review may result in revisions which would
                               have the effect of lowering the March-April rise in seasonally adjusted retail
                               trade and total nonagricultural employment.
                                   Revisions as necessary will appear in the June issue of this report.

                                                                  Technical Note

                                                      Additional information concerning the preparation
                                                      of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                                      and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                                      survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                                      technical notes for each of these series, available
                                                      from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.



                   INTRODUCTION                                                        Relation between the household and payroll series

                       The statistics in this periodical are compiled from                 The household and payroll data supplement one
                   three major sources*. (1) household interviews, (2) pay-            another, each providing significant types of information
                   roll reports from employers, and (3) administrative                 that the other cannot suitably supply. Population charac-
                   statistics of unemployment insurance systems.                       teristics, for example, are readily obtained only from

                                                                Establishment Data
                   COLLECTION                                                          on Labor Turnover. These schedules are of the "shuttle"
                                                                                       type, with space for each month of the calendar year.
                      Payroll reports provide current information on wage              The schedule is returned to the respondent each month
                   and salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turn-             by the collecting agency so that the next month's data
                   over in nonfarm establishments, by industry and geo-                can be entered. This procedure assures maximum
                   graphic location.                                                   comparability and accuracy of reporting, since the

                                                                 SEASONAL ADJUSTMENT

                       Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring         directly to the corresponding unadjusted series, but
                   seasonal movement which can be estimated on the basis              seasonally adjusted employment totals for all employees
                   of past experience. By eliminating that part of the change         and production workers by industry divisions are ob-
                   which can be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is           tained by summing the seasonally adjusted data which
